questions.text: I find that my instinct when receiving a gift is to think about who else could use it.
I read social subtext as naturally as I read printed text.
I feel that having a single decision-maker leads to clearer outcomes.
When I have a vision, I pursue it whether or not others initially agree.
I find that my emotional state can shift dramatically within the span of minutes.
I sequence my tasks so that each one builds logically on the one before.
I often learn about social conflicts well after they have already been resolved.
I feel that the moral demand of suffering is what prevents me from becoming complacent about ethics.
I find that doing without, so that others can have, brings me a quiet joy.
I feel that I was born with a naturally calm nervous system.
I feel that investing in my appearance, health, and skills is time well spent.
I find that I am able to process even the most distressing accounts of human suffering without my emotional stability being compromised.
I take pride in my reputation for dependability and follow-through.
I often know what someone is about to say before they say it based on context cues.
I believe that the obligation to respond to suffering is a moral demand whose urgency does not diminish with time, distance, or the number of times I have encountered it.
I reschedule plans without guilt when something more important or interesting comes up.
I prefer a supportive role in negotiations and high-stakes conversations.
I have a vivid inner emotional landscape that is constantly shifting.
